cards.title: BNYS History Taking — Naturopathic Framework

alerts.label: Naturopathic perspective
alerts.text: The aim is to understand the WHOLE person — physical, mental, emotional, social, and spiritual dimensions — not just the disease. Always ask: what is obstructing the body's innate healing force (Vis Medicatrix Naturae)?

pearls.label: 🟡 Clinical Pearl
pearls.text: The naturopathic history is often more revealing than lab tests. A patient with chronic fatigue who sleeps 5 hours, eats processed food, never exercises, and has high stress doesn't need a diagnosis — they need a lifestyle prescription. The history IS the diagnosis in naturopathy.

steps.title: Chief complaint (CC)
steps.text: — In patient's own words. Duration, onset, progression. Previous episodes. Previous treatments (allopathic + naturopathic).

steps.title: Dietary history
steps.text: — Meal frequency, types of food (raw/cooked/processed/non-veg), eating habits (rushed/mindful), water intake (L/day), tea/coffee/alcohol intake, fasting history, appetite, digestion, bowel habits

steps.title: Sleep history
steps.text: — Hours, quality (restful/disturbed), dreams, sleep time, position, snoring, daytime drowsiness

steps.title: Physical activity
steps.text: — Type, frequency, duration. Occupation (sedentary/active). Exercise tolerance. Any yoga/meditation practice?

steps.title: Mental & emotional status
steps.text: — Stress levels (work/personal), anxiety, depression, anger, emotional triggers, relationships, recent life events

steps.title: Elimination history
steps.text: — Bowel frequency (ideal = once daily, morning), consistency, colour. Urinary frequency, colour, odour. Sweating pattern.

steps.title: Sunlight & fresh air exposure
steps.text: — Time outdoors, sunlight access, ventilation at home/work

steps.title: Social & environmental history
steps.text: — Occupation, pollution exposure, smoking/alcohol/tobacco, economic stress, social support

steps.title: ICE — Ideas, Concerns, Expectations
steps.text: — What does the patient think is causing this? What are they most worried about? What do they expect from naturopathic treatment?

cards.title: Vitality Assessment — The Naturopathic Lens

pearls.label: 🟡 Clinical Pearl
pearls.text: In naturopathy, symptoms are not the enemy — they are the body's healing attempts. Fever, acute inflammation, mucus discharge, diarrhoea = body trying to eliminate. Support the process, don't suppress it (unless the patient is in danger). This is the fundamental philosophical difference from allopathy.

cards._legacyHtml:
Vital force (Vis Medicatrix Naturae) — Assess overall energy, resilience, and healing capacity. High vitality = brisk healing response. Low vitality = slow, poor response, tendency to chronicity.
Tongue assessment — Coated (white = toxin accumulation, digestive sluggishness), pale (anaemia, cold constitution), red/dry (heat, dehydration), geographic (stress, nutritional deficiency)
Iris (iridology — supplementary) — Zone assessment. Note as supplementary finding, not primary diagnosis. Always correlate with clinical findings.
Pulse quality — Rate, rhythm, strength, volume. In naturopathy, also note: tense/relaxed, superficial/deep, fast/slow — reflects nervous system state.
Skin quality — Lustre, moisture, turgor, temperature, colour. Skin is a mirror of internal health. Dry skin = dehydration/poor fat intake. Sallow = liver sluggishness.
Elimination efficiency — Bowel (ideal: once daily, Bristol type 3–4), urine (pale yellow = well hydrated), sweating (natural detox). Poor elimination = toxin accumulation = disease tendency.
🟡 Clinical Pearl
In naturopathy, symptoms are not the enemy — they are the body's healing attempts. Fever, acute inflammation, mucus discharge, diarrhoea = body trying to eliminate. Support the process, don't suppress it (unless the patient is in danger). This is the fundamental philosophical difference from allopathy.

rows: Height, weight, BMI, waist circumference, waist-hip ratio

Baseline for monitoring treatment response. Hypertension management target.

pearls.label: 🟡 Clinical Pearl
pearls.text: Always assess the patient's READINESS for change. A patient who isn't ready to modify their diet will not benefit from a perfect naturopathic prescription. The Stages of Change model (precontemplation → contemplation → preparation → action → maintenance) guides how you frame your advice.

cards.title: BNYS Treatment Planning Framework

alerts.label: BNYS treatment prescription components
alerts.text: Diet plan (individualised) · Fasting schedule · Daily yoga protocol · Pranayama routine · Hydrotherapy prescriptions · Massage schedule · Acupressure points to self-apply · Sleep hygiene · Sunlight prescription · Stress management · Exercise protocol · Follow-up interval

steps.title: Remove obstacles to cure
steps.text: — Identify and eliminate factors obstructing healing: poor diet, sedentary behaviour, stress, toxic exposures, bad sleep

steps.title: Support natural healing
steps.text: — Use the least invasive intervention first: diet → lifestyle → exercise → yoga → hydrotherapy → massage → acupressure → other modalities

steps.title: Treat the whole person
steps.text: — Address physical, mental, emotional, social, and spiritual dimensions simultaneously

steps.title: Identify and treat root cause
steps.text: — Not just symptoms. Ask: WHY has this person developed this condition at this time?

steps.title: Prevention and education
steps.text: — Every consultation is a teaching opportunity. Empower patients to maintain health independently.

steps.title: Doctor as teacher (Docere)
steps.text: — Educate patient about health principles. The best outcome is a patient who no longer needs you.

cards.title: Yoga Nidra — Systematic Relaxation

alerts.label: Evidence base
alerts.text: Yoga Nidra has the strongest evidence of any relaxation technique: reduces cortisol, improves HbA1c in T2DM (Amita et al 2009), reduces anxiety scores (HAMA scale), improves sleep quality. 1 hour of Yoga Nidra = equivalent deep rest to 4 hours of sleep (EEG studies show theta/delta wave induction).

steps.title: Setup (5 min)
steps.text: — Shavasana position, warm blanket, eye pillow. Set Sankalpa (short positive intention, repeated 3 times at start and end). Room slightly darkened, no distractions.

steps.title: Physical relaxation (10 min)
steps.text: — Systematic rotation of consciousness through body parts (61-point relaxation). Instructor speaks slowly, 3–5 seconds per body part.

steps.title: Breath awareness (5 min)
steps.text: — Count breaths backwards from 54 to 1. If mind wanders, restart from 54.

steps.title: Opposites (5 min)
steps.text: — Experience pairs: heavy/light, warm/cold, pain/pleasure, joy/sadness. Rapidly alternating to develop equanimity.

steps.title: Visualisation (10 min)
steps.text: — Rapid image sequences. Then a stable, pleasant scene (personalised). Activates REM-like brainwave states.

steps.title: Conclusion (5 min)
steps.text: — Repeat Sankalpa. Slowly bring awareness back. Roll to right side. Sit up slowly.

cards.title: Massage Techniques — BNYS Fundamentals

rows: Long, gliding strokes with palmar surface, toward heart. Light to moderate pressure. Used to begin and end session.
Increases venous/lymphatic return, warms tissue, relaxation, reduces oedema
Oedema ★, anxiety, fatigue, post-exercise recovery, introduction to session
Kneading, wringing, rolling of muscles between hands. Lifts and compresses tissue.
Breaks adhesions, improves circulation, reduces muscle tension, removes metabolic waste

Rhythmic hacking, cupping, clapping, tapping. Brisk, stimulating movements.

Chest physiotherapy ★ (bronchiectasis, COPD), sports warm-up, muscle atrophy, low energy
Deep circular or transverse movements on specific areas. Thumb/fingertip pressure.
Breaks down adhesions, scar tissue, chronic muscle knots (trigger points)

Fine trembling movements transmitted through hands to patient's tissue.
Relaxes spasm, stimulates nerve endings, loosens secretions

Very light superficial touch, slow speed. Over-clothing or on skin.

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: Always start with effleurage (warms tissue, builds trust, assesses tone) and end with effleurage (closes the session, promotes venous return). The session arc: effleurage → petrissage → friction (specific areas) → tapotement (if indicated) → effleurage to close. Total duration: 45–60 minutes for full body.

cards.title: Contraindications to Massage

alerts.label: Relative contraindications — proceed with caution and modified technique
alerts.text: Pregnancy (avoid abdomen, avoid deep pressure, avoid certain positions) · Osteoporosis (avoid heavy tapotement) · Varicose veins (light effleurage only, avoid direct pressure) · Uncontrolled hypertension · Medications: anticoagulants (bruising risk)

cards._legacyHtml:
⚠️ ABSOLUTE contraindications — do NOT massage
Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) — Risk of dislodging clot → pulmonary embolism. Any unilateral leg swelling, calf pain, redness = DVT until proven otherwise. DO NOT MASSAGE.
Active infection / fever — Massage increases circulation and can spread local infection systemically. Viral fever: absolute rest, not massage.
Acute inflammation — Active arthritis flare, acute injury (RICE protocol instead), acute appendicitis, active phlebitis
Malignancy (over tumour site) — Risk of promoting metastasis via lymphatic stimulation. Generalised massage with caution only in palliative care after oncology consultation.
Open wounds, burns, skin conditions — Eczema (acute weeping), infected wounds, third-degree burns, contagious skin conditions
Recent fractures or surgery — Until cleared by treating physician
Severe cardiac conditions — Acute MI, severe heart failure, acute arrhythmia
Bleeding disorders / anticoagulants — Deep petrissage/friction can cause bruising and haematoma
Relative contraindications — proceed with caution and modified technique
Pregnancy (avoid abdomen, avoid deep pressure, avoid certain positions) · Osteoporosis (avoid heavy tapotement) · Varicose veins (light effleurage only, avoid direct pressure) · Uncontrolled hypertension · Medications: anticoagulants (bruising risk)

cards.title: Diaphragmatic Breathing — Foundation Technique

alerts.label: Why this matters
alerts.text: 90% of patients breathe thoracically (chest only), using only 1/3 of lung capacity. Diaphragmatic breathing activates the vagus nerve → parasympathetic response → reduces cortisol, HR, and BP. It is the single most powerful naturopathic intervention that takes <2 minutes to teach.

steps.title: Position
steps.text: — Supine or seated. One hand on chest, one on abdomen (navel level).

steps.title: Teach the movement
steps.text: — "Breathe in so that ONLY the hand on the abdomen rises. The chest hand should barely move."

steps.title: Ratio
steps.text: — Inhale 4 counts, exhale 6–8 counts. Extended exhale activates parasympathetic system most effectively.

steps.title: Practice
steps.text: — 5–10 minutes morning + evening. Can be done in any position, any time (before meals, before sleep, during stress).

cards.title: Naturopathic Dietary Principles

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: The most powerful single dietary change for most patients: replace white rice/bread/sugar with whole grains and increase vegetables to 50% of every plate. For Indian patients: reduce oil quantity (from 5 tbsp/day to 2), eliminate packaged snacks, and shift to brown rice or millets. These three changes alone address >70% of common metabolic disorders.

cards._legacyHtml:
Whole foods, predominantly plant-based — Fruits, vegetables, legumes, nuts, seeds, whole grains as the foundation. Raw-to-cooked ratio ideally 40:60 (raw foods retain enzymes, vitamins, phytonutrients).
Elimination diet approach — Remove common irritants first: refined sugar, processed foods, trans fats, excessive dairy, alcohol, excess caffeine. Assess response before adding back.
Food combining principles — Proteins + starches = poor digestion (avoid eating meat + rice together). Fruits eaten alone or 30 min before meals. Avoid cold water/drinks with meals (impairs digestive enzymes).
Meal timing — Breakfast = moderate, Lunch = largest meal (digestive fire peak), Dinner = light, early (by 7 pm ideally). No eating within 3 hours of sleep.
Water therapy — 6–8 glasses/day. 2 glasses warm water first thing morning (stimulates peristalsis, flushes kidneys). Water 30 min before or 90 min after meals (not during — dilutes digestive enzymes).
Mindful eating — Eat slowly (20 min per meal). Chew each bite 20–30 times. No screens/phone during meals. Gratitude before eating. Eat only when genuinely hungry.
🟡 Pearl

cards.title: Fasting Monitoring Protocol

alerts.label: ⚠️ Absolute contraindications to fasting
alerts.text: Type 1 diabetes (risk of DKA) · Pregnancy/lactation · Severe underweight (BMI <17) · Active eating disorder · Recent major surgery · Active TB or severe infection · Severe cardiac failure · Children <14 years · Patients on insulin (require medical supervision and dose adjustment)

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: Refeeding after fasting is as important as the fast itself. NEVER break a fast with a heavy meal. Rule: number of days fasted = number of days of gradual refeeding. Break with diluted fruit juice (50:50 water) → whole fruit → cooked vegetables → normal food. Refeeding syndrome (hypophosphataemia) risk exists after extended fasting.

steps.title: Pre-fast assessment
steps.text: — BP, HR, weight, blood glucose (capillary), urine ketones baseline. Review medications (especially insulin, antidiabetics, antihypertensives — all may need dose reduction during fasting).

steps.title: Daily monitoring during fast
steps.text: — BP morning and evening (hypotension is the most common complication), capillary glucose (if diabetic — check BD), urine output (should be 1.5–2L/day), ketone level (urine strips), weight (daily), sleep, mental state

steps.title: Expected fasting symptoms (normal)
steps.text: — Headache (day 1–2: glucose withdrawal), hunger (peaks day 2, reduces day 3), mild weakness, light-headedness on standing (orthostatic hypotension — managed with adequate water), slight bad breath (ketosis)

steps.title: Stop fasting immediately if:
steps.text: BP <90/60, blood glucose <3.5 mmol/L, persistent vomiting, fainting, severe cardiac symptoms, altered consciousness, signs of dehydration despite water intake

cards.title: Type 2 Diabetes — Naturopathic Management

alerts.label: Evidence base

cards.title: Obesity — Naturopathic Management

alerts.label: Naturopathic approach to obesity
alerts.text: Obesity is not a willpower problem — it is a hormonal, metabolic, and behavioural disorder with strong environmental drivers. The naturopathic approach addresses ALL root causes simultaneously, not just calorie counting.

rows: Reduce daily calories by 500–750 kcal deficit. Increase protein (preserve muscle), high fibre (satiety), reduce refined carbs + sugar + liquid calories (biggest impact). No ultra-processed foods.
150–300 min/week aerobic + 2× resistance training. Start where the patient is — even 10 min walks count. Increase gradually. NEAT (Non-exercise activity thermogenesis) — standing, walking, stairs.
Surya Namaskar (10–12 rounds/day = 350–400 kcal), Kapalbhati (boosts metabolism), dynamic sequences
Largest meal = lunch. No eating after 7pm. 16:8 intermittent fasting. Eat slowly (brain takes 20 min to register fullness).
7–8 hrs/night. Sleep deprivation → ghrelin ↑ (hunger hormone) + leptin ↓ (satiety hormone) → increased appetite by 300 kcal/day
Cortisol drives central obesity specifically (belly fat). Yoga Nidra, meditation, CBT approaches

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: Measure waist circumference, not just weight. Waist >90cm (M) / >80cm (F) in Indians = central obesity = high metabolic risk (Indian-specific cut-offs lower than international). A patient may be normal BMI but have metabolic obesity (TOFI — thin outside, fat inside). Visceral fat is the dangerous fat, not subcutaneous fat.

cards.title: Chronic Constipation Protocol

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: The squatting position for defecation (Malasana) is physiologically superior to sitting on a Western toilet — it straightens the anorectal angle and reduces straining. Recommend a toilet stool (Squatty Potty) or teach Malasana. This alone resolves many cases of chronic constipation and straining.

steps.title: Diet
steps.text: — 2.5L water/day (most important) + fibre 25–35g/day (fruits, vegetables, whole grains, isabgol 1 tsp with warm water at night), soaked prunes (2–4 at bedtime), papaya (daily)

steps.title: Morning routine
steps.text: — Drink 2 glasses warm water immediately on waking → stimulates gastrocolic reflex → natural urge within 20–30 min. Never suppress the urge.

steps.title: Yoga
steps.text: — Pavanmuktasana series (10 rounds), Ardha Matsyendrasana, Dhanurasana, Malasana (squat pose — best physiological position for defecation)

steps.title: Abdominal massage
steps.text: — Castor oil massage clockwise (in direction of colon), 5–10 min, morning. Stimulates peristalsis.

steps.title: Hydrotherapy
steps.text: — Cold hip bath 15–20 min daily, or cold compress on abdomen — stimulates intestinal activity

steps.title: Pranayama
steps.text: — Kapalbhati 5–10 min daily (powerful abdominal pump — natural peristalsis stimulator)

cards.title: Insomnia Protocol

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: CBT-I (Cognitive Behavioural Therapy for Insomnia) has more evidence than any sleeping medication for chronic insomnia (AASM guidelines). BNYS physicians can implement the behavioural components (sleep restriction therapy, stimulus control, sleep hygiene) — this is within scope and highly effective.

steps.title: Sleep hygiene (most important)
steps.text: — Fixed sleep/wake time (even weekends). Bedroom = only sleep + sex (no screens). Temperature 18–20°C. Dark room. No screen 1 hour before bed. No caffeine after 2pm.

steps.title: Yoga Nidra
steps.text: — 30 min before bed. Most effective single naturopathic intervention for insomnia. Can use recorded guided practice.

steps.title: Pranayama
steps.text: — 4-7-8 breathing (inhale 4, hold 7, exhale 8) OR Anulom Vilom 10 min before bed. Activates parasympathetic system.

steps.title: Acupressure
steps.text: — KD1 (sole of foot), HT7 (wrist), SP6 (above medial ankle) — 2 min each before sleep. Self-applicable.

steps.title: Diet
steps.text: — Warm milk (tryptophan) + 1/4 tsp nutmeg (natural sedative) at bedtime. Light dinner. Banana (magnesium + tryptophan). Avoid heavy meals within 3 hours of sleep.

steps.title: Warm bath/foot soak
steps.text: — 20 min warm bath 1–2 hours before bed. The subsequent drop in core body temperature triggers sleep onset.

cards.title: Yoga & Exercise Interactions with Medications

alerts.label: ⚠️ Critical interactions — always check medications before prescribing yoga/exercise

rows: Exercise + yoga (especially inversions) can lower BP further → hypotension, dizziness, syncope
Monitor BP before and after sessions. Avoid Shirshasana, prolonged inversions. Check for orthostatic hypotension on standing.
Blunt tachycardia response to exercise — HR won't rise normally. Can't use HR to guide exercise intensity.
Use RPE (Rating of Perceived Exertion) scale 1–10 instead of heart rate. Target RPE 5–7.
Exercise lowers blood glucose — risk of hypoglycaemia during or after session
Check glucose before yoga. If <5.5 mmol/L: small snack first. Carry glucose tablets/juice. Never practice on empty stomach if on insulin.
Dehydration risk during exercise + hot yoga. Electrolyte imbalance (K+, Mg) causes cramps, arrhythmia.
Extra hydration before/during. Check electrolytes if on loop diuretics. Avoid hot yoga.
Contact-type massage contraindicated (bruising, haematoma). Hot wax treatments increase absorption of topical drugs.
Use light effleurage only. No deep friction/petrissage. No cupping therapy.
Osteoporosis risk — avoid forceful spinal twists, extreme ranges of motion. Muscle weakness (steroid myopathy).
Gentle yoga only. No weight-bearing inversions. Screen BMD before intensive yoga.
Synergistic BP lowering with exercise. Risk of hypotension especially in hot conditions.
Monitor BP. Ensure adequate hydration. Avoid hot yoga or hot rooms.

cards.title: Motivational Interviewing — Core Skills

alerts.label: What is MI?
alerts.text: Motivational Interviewing is a collaborative, goal-oriented conversation style that helps patients find their own motivation for change. It is directive but patient-centred. Evidence shows it doubles compliance rates compared to prescriptive advice-giving.

pearls.label: 🟡 Clinical Pearl
pearls.text: The "readiness ruler" technique: "On a scale of 0–10, how important is this change to you? And how confident are you that you could make it?" Low importance → explore values and consequences. Low confidence → explore barriers and small achievable steps. This one question set saves 20 minutes of ineffective advice-giving.

steps.title: Open questions
steps.text: — "What concerns do you have about your health?" "What would your life look like if you made this change?" Avoid yes/no questions. Open questions = you learn, they reflect.

steps.title: Affirmations
steps.text: — Acknowledge strengths, efforts, and past successes. "You've managed to quit smoking once before — that shows real determination." Builds self-efficacy.

steps.title: Reflective listening
steps.text: — Reflect back what you hear: "So it sounds like you know what you should do, but finding the time feels impossible?" This feels deeply validating to patients.

steps.title: Summaries
steps.text: — Periodically summarise what you've heard: "Let me see if I've understood correctly..." This helps patients hear their own situation more clearly.

cards.title: Stages of Change — Transtheoretical Model

rows: "I don't have a problem" / "My diet is fine"
Raise awareness gently. Plant a seed. Don't confront — it increases resistance.
"What do you know about how diet affects your condition?"
Explore ambivalence. Tip the decisional balance. Highlight discrepancy between values and behaviour.
"What would be different in your life if you made this change?"
Help plan. Set specific, achievable SMART goals. Address anticipated barriers.
"What is the first small step you could take this week?"
Provide support, practical tools, monitor progress. Celebrate small wins.
"How are you finding the changes so far? What's working well?"
Help identify triggers for relapse. Build self-efficacy. Prevent complacency.
"What situations make it hardest to maintain the changes?"
Normalise relapse — it's part of change. Re-engage with compassion, not judgement.
"Most people have some setbacks — what did you learn from this?"

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: Matching your intervention to the patient's stage of change is the most important counselling skill. Giving detailed dietary advice to a precontemplation patient = wasted effort + patient feels judged and doesn't return. Checking readiness first saves time and builds the therapeutic relationship.

RelapseReturns to old behaviourNormalise relapse — it's part of change. Re-engage with compassion, not judgement."Most people have some setbacks — what did you learn from this?"
🟡 Pearl
Matching your intervention to the patient's stage of change is the most important counselling skill. Giving detailed dietary advice to a precontemplation patient = wasted effort + patient feels judged and doesn't return. Checking readiness first saves time and builds the therapeutic relationship.

cards.title: Lifestyle Prescription — SMART Goal Setting

alerts.label: SMART goals in naturopathic prescriptions
alerts.text: Vague advice ("eat better, exercise more") is never followed. SMART goals are: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound.

rows: "Add one cup of raw vegetables (cucumber, carrot, tomato) to lunch every day for the next 2 weeks."
"Walk briskly for 20 minutes after dinner, 5 days this week. Track it in a notebook."
"Practice Yoga Nidra recording (45 min) every day before sleep for 30 days. Note quality of sleep each morning."
"Drink 2 glasses warm water immediately on waking each morning, and keep a 1L bottle visible at your desk. Target 2L by 3pm daily."
"Practice the 6 asanas I taught you for 20 minutes every morning before breakfast, 6 days a week. Review in 4 weeks."
"Replace your afternoon tea (2 tsp sugar) with green tea (no sugar) from Monday. One change at a time."

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: One change at a time. Patients who are given 10 lifestyle changes at once make zero. Agree on ONE priority change with the patient (not what you think is most important — what THEY are most ready to change). Master that first. Then add the next. This approach has much higher long-term compliance.

cards.title: ABSOLUTE CONTRAINDICATIONS — Do NOT Treat Naturopathically

alerts.label: ⚠️ These are emergencies — refer immediately, do not attempt naturopathic treatment first

cards._legacyHtml:
⚠️ These are emergencies — refer immediately, do not attempt naturopathic treatment first
Acute myocardial infarction (Heart attack) — Chest pain + radiation + sweating + breathlessness = call 108/112. No yoga, massage, or any treatment. Immediate MONA + referral.
Acute stroke — Sudden face drooping, arm weakness, speech difficulty, severe headache, vision loss = FAST response. Immediate emergency referral. Time is brain (1.9 million neurons die per minute).
Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) — T1DM or T2DM with vomiting, abdominal pain, ketones, deep breathing (Kussmaul), altered consciousness. Medical emergency. IV insulin + fluids needed.
Hypertensive emergency — BP ≥180/120 with end-organ damage (headache, visual changes, chest pain, neurological deficit). Medical emergency — IV medication required. Do NOT attempt to lower BP with yoga/breathing alone.
Acute surgical abdomen — Peritonitis, appendicitis, bowel perforation, ectopic pregnancy. Board-like rigidity, rebound tenderness, absent bowel sounds. Surgical emergency.
Sepsis/Septic shock — Fever + hypotension + tachycardia + altered consciousness. Life-threatening. IV antibiotics within 1 hour. Not a naturopathic condition.
Active pulmonary tuberculosis (TB) — Confirm diagnosis first. Naturopathy can be COMPLEMENTARY to anti-TB drugs (RIPE protocol), never sole treatment. Highly contagious — use appropriate precautions.
Acute asthma attack (severe) — Cannot complete sentences, SpO₂ <92%, RR >30. Bronchodilators + O₂ required. Yoga/pranayama are for prevention, not acute attack management.
Psychiatric emergency — Active suicidal ideation, psychosis (hallucinations, delusions, disorganised behaviour), severe mania. Immediate psychiatric referral.
Suspected malignancy — Unexplained weight loss, night sweats, persistent lymphadenopathy, haemoptysis, rectal/vaginal bleeding, suspicious breast lump. Urgent referral for diagnosis first. Do NOT delay diagnosis with naturopathic treatment.
Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) / Pulmonary embolism — Unilateral calf pain + swelling (DVT) or sudden breathlessness + pleuritic chest pain (PE). No massage. Immediate anticoagulation needed.

cards.title: Red Flag Symptoms — Always Investigate Before Treatment

alerts.label: Red flags in BNYS practice — investigate or refer before treating naturopathically

rows: Age <20 or >55, bilateral leg weakness/numbness, saddle anaesthesia, bladder/bowel dysfunction, fever, unexplained weight loss, history of malignancy
Cauda equina syndrome (emergency), spinal cord compression, metastatic malignancy, spinal infection
Thunderclap onset (worst headache of life), progressive, on waking, associated neurological signs, fever + neck stiffness, head injury
Subarachnoid haemorrhage, raised ICP, meningitis, intracranial tumour
Central, crushing, radiating to arm/jaw, associated breathlessness, sweating, nausea
Acute MI, PE, aortic dissection, unstable angina
Unexplained, >5% in 3 months, without dietary change
Malignancy, TB, hyperthyroidism, diabetes, HIV, malabsorption
>39°C, prolonged (>7 days), with night sweats, rigor, localised pain/redness
Serious infection (malaria, TB, typhoid, IE, abscess), lymphoma
Sudden onset, at rest, rapidly progressive, associated chest pain or haemoptysis
PE, pneumothorax, acute pulmonary oedema, severe asthma
Rectal bleeding especially dark/melaena, dysphagia progressive, vomiting blood, change in bowel habit >6 weeks in >40 yrs
GI malignancy, peptic ulcer, GI bleed
Hepatitis (infective, alcoholic), biliary obstruction, malignancy — requires liver function tests + imaging

pearls.label: 🟡 Critical Pearl
pearls.text: Your most important clinical skill as a BNYS physician is knowing what is BEYOND your scope. A doctor who refers appropriately and promptly saves lives. A doctor who tries to treat everything with naturopathy harms patients. The two most important questions before starting any naturopathic treatment: (1) Is this an emergency? (2) Do I need to rule out a serious underlying cause first?

cards.title: Conditions Where Naturopathy is Complementary (Not Primary)

alerts.label: Collaborative approach — naturopathy supports but does not replace allopathic treatment

rows: Yoga + diet to improve glucose control, reduce insulin requirements. Never reduce insulin without endocrinologist involvement.
Yoga Nidra, stress management to reduce seizure frequency. Avoid fasting, avoid Kapalbhati/Bhastrika (may trigger seizure).
Yoga, pranayama as ADJUNCT under psychiatric supervision. Never advise stopping psychiatric medications.
Nutritional support, pranayama (breathing exercises), lifestyle support. COMPLEMENTARY ONLY.
Nutritional optimisation, yoga to improve quality of life and immunity. Never advise stopping ART.
Gentle yoga (modified), pranayama, dietary sodium restriction. No intensive yoga or hydrotherapy.
Nutritional support, Yoga Nidra for anxiety and pain, massage for comfort (not over tumour site). Integrative oncology consultation needed.
Prenatal yoga (specific), Bhramari pranayama, dietary support. Avoid SP6, LI4, SP8 acupressure points. Refer any complication immediately.

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: The phrase to use with patients: "Naturopathy and modern medicine are not enemies — they are partners. My job is to support your body's healing while you also get the treatment you need from your specialist. I will coordinate with your other doctors." This builds trust, keeps patients safe, and positions BNYS as the professional discipline it is.
